opp.ais_why:
• Gajendra Badodekar, Director of Global Sourcing, anchored the March 26 2026 demo on AI-driven automation across CLM, AP and analytics. Legal lead Karishma Muravne probed AI-identified clause export mechanics and admin configuration for global standards. AP lead Marci Jasinski engaged AP Smartdesk's AI extraction, fraud and duplicate checks. AI importance is rated Very Critical.
• Twenty-one action items came out of the March 26 demo, several of them AI-specific: confirming AI-driven clause search exports include clause text, demonstrating CLM admin configuration for AI playbook controls, and adoption benchmarks for AI modules by region and BFSI industry. The buyer's framing of marking up documents focused squarely on understanding the use of AI.
• Russell is replacing fragmented Agiloft, Concur and spreadsheets with a unified Source-to-Pay including AI-driven clause search, contract risk analysis and autonomous negotiation. Three substantive sessions in March and April 2026 with the full procurement, legal and AP leadership, and an internal vendor shortlist meeting scheduled for the following week. AI is a buyer-stated need and the RFX is imminent.

metrics.narrative: Russell Investments articulated quantified pain across three operational areas during discovery and scoping calls. First, procurement inefficiency: Gajendra stated on 2026-02-17 that current P2P processes involve heavy manual effort and cited 'we need to reduce cycle time and manual touchpoints' when describing requisition-to-PO workflows. Second, contract risk and compliance gaps: Karishma (legal/contracts) on 2026-06-16 emphasized the need for AI-assisted redlining and clause standardization to reduce legal review bottlenecks, asking specifically for a live demo of AI redlining assistance to validate time savings. Third, spend visibility: the evaluation team requested region-specific (Americas, EMEA, APAC) and BFSI industry adoption benchmarks for spend analytics on 2026-03-26, signaling a requirement to quantify ROI and benchmark against peers. While cycle-time reduction and legal-review savings were articulated qualitatively, no stakeholder provided a hard baseline metric (e.g., current contract-review hours per month, current PO cycle time in days, or annual manual-process cost). The pain is confirmed and operational, but the business case has not been fully quantified in stakeholder language. Gajendra committed to preparing an internal business case after receiving NDA-bound ROI materials from Zycus (2026-03-26), but delivery confirmation is not on record.

champion.narrative: Gajendra Badodekar is the confirmed champion, active and driving the evaluation process, but his sponsorship is at the manager/senior-analyst level and he lacks the authority to approve budget or close the commercial decision independently. Evidence of championship: (1) Gajendra has been present and leading on all five Avoma calls from discovery (2026-02-17) through final AI validation (2026-06-16), consistently framing the business problem, asking detailed product questions, and coordinating the cross-functional evaluation team. (2) He took ownership of the vendor-selection timeline, stating on 2026-03-26 'I need to wrap up vendor selection by mid-July' and reaffirming the July 15 shortlist deadline in the Next Step (2026-06-24). (3) He drove internal process steps: committing to share NDAs with shortlisted vendors, requesting NDA-bound ROI business cases and implementation plans from Zycus, and organizing use-case validation sessions with legal (Karishma) and AP (Janice). (4) He multi-threaded the evaluation team, bringing in legal, AP, finance, and additional procurement stakeholders across calls. However, Gajendra has NOT introduced Zycus to the Economic Buyer, CFO, or any VP/C-level executive, and on no call has he described the budget-approval process or named the final commercial decision-maker. The Next Step (2026-06-24) describes him as 'Champion' but notes he 'needs to wrap up vendor selection,' implying his mandate ends at the shortlist recommendation and he must escalate for final approval. His engagement is active (last call 2026-06-16, proposal submitted 2026-06-23, shortlist target 2026-07-15), but his access to the Economic Buyer is unproven and his ability to close the deal independently is zero. This is a strong operational champion with a critical gap: he cannot unlock budget without executive sponsorship, and that path is not yet visible.

competition.narrative: Russell Investments is running a competitive multi-vendor evaluation with Agiloft confirmed as the primary competitor and Concur as a secondary or adjacent consideration for Expense/AP scope. Agiloft is named in the Salesforce Competitors__c field and was mentioned by stakeholders on the 2026-03-26 call when discussing CLM requirements and vendor-shortlist composition. The evaluation team's questions about AI redlining, clause extraction, and contract standardization (Karishma's focus on 2026-06-16) directly map to Agiloft's CLM and AI contract-review positioning, indicating they are being evaluated for the iContract and Merlin scope head-to-head with Zycus. Concur was referenced as the incumbent or consideration for Expense/AP workflows (Janice's questions on 2026-04-28 about invoice matching and payment processes suggest Russell may be running SAP Concur or evaluating it for the iSaaS/Expense scope). The competitive dynamic: Agiloft is strong in CLM and contract AI but weaker in upstream P2P and spend analytics, so the Zycus wedge is the end-to-end platform story (P2P + CLM + Spend Analytics + Merlin AI in one suite vs. Agiloft CLM + a separate P2P tool). The activation: position Merlin's AI redlining and Intake's agentic sourcing workflows as differentiation, demonstrate live (not roadmap) AI capability in the final validation sessions, and emphasize total-cost-of-ownership and integration simplicity vs. a multi-vendor stack. Risk: if Karishma (legal) and the contracts team prioritize best-of-breed CLM depth over platform breadth, Agiloft may win the CLM decision and Zycus may be shortlisted only for P2P, fragmenting the deal. The Next Step (2026-06-24) does not mention competitive positioning or a recent competitive review, so it is unclear whether Zycus has run a formal competitive briefing with Gajendra in the final stretch.

identify_pain.narrative: Russell Investments has articulated operational and compliance pain across three functional areas, each owned by a named stakeholder. First, procurement inefficiency and manual workflows: Gajendra Badodekar (procurement lead and champion) stated on the 2026-02-17 discovery call that 'current P2P processes involve heavy manual effort' and emphasized the need to 'reduce cycle time and manual touchpoints in requisition-to-PO workflows.' This pain is procurement-owned and is the primary driver for the P2P (iSource, iSave) and analytics (ML iAnalyze) scope. Second, contract-management risk and legal review bottlenecks: Karishma Muravne (legal/contracts) on 2026-06-16 described the pain point as slow and inconsistent contract redlining, asking specifically for a live demo of AI-assisted redlining and clause extraction to validate time savings. She stated the team needs to 'standardize global clause libraries and reduce legal review cycle time,' indicating that contracts currently lack automation and are a bottleneck in vendor onboarding and renewals. This pain is legal-owned and drives the CLM (iContract) and Merlin (AI redlining) scope. Third, accounts-payable and invoice-matching inefficiency: Janice (AP) on 2026-04-28 asked detailed questions about 3-way invoice matching, payment workflows, and supplier-portal email-reply support, signaling that AP processes are manual and integration with the ERP/finance system is incomplete. This pain is finance/AP-owned and drives the AP automation and Expense (iSaaS) scope. All three pain areas are confirmed and stakeholder-owned, but none has been quantified with a hard baseline metric (e.g., current contract-review hours per month, current PO cycle time in days, or invoice-exception rate). The pain is real and operational, but the business case remains qualitative rather than ROI-anchored.

paper_process.narrative: The contracting and paper process is partially mapped but critical steps remain unconfirmed. On 2026-03-26 Gajendra committed to 'sharing NDAs with shortlisted vendors' and Zycus committed to preparing 'NDA-bound ROI business cases, implementation plans, and deeper demos' for delivery by early April. However, NDA execution is not confirmed in any subsequent call, Next Step, or Task, and the ROI business case delivery is likewise unverified. If the NDA has not been signed, Zycus may not be able to share the detailed commercial and implementation materials Gajendra needs to build his internal business case and shortlist recommendation. Beyond NDA, no stakeholder has discussed MSA/SOW structure, legal redlining process, InfoSec review requirements, or procurement-channel mechanics (e.g., whether a GPO, sourcing consultant, or direct procurement team manages contracting). The Next Step (2026-06-24) references proposal submission and shortlist target but does not mention legal, InfoSec, or contracting next steps. Given the $120K deal size and the P2P/CLM scope (which typically triggers InfoSec, data-privacy, and ERP-integration reviews), a 30-45 day legal/contracting cycle is standard. With close date 2026-07-17 (17 days out) and vendor-selection not finalized until July 15, the paper process is structurally compressed to the point of impossibility unless MSA/SOW negotiation and InfoSec review run in parallel starting immediately. The visibility gap: we do not know if Russell's legal and InfoSec teams have begun their diligence, and we do not know if a standard MSA template or custom redlining will be required.

decision_process.narrative: Russell Investments is running a formal multi-vendor RFP/shortlist process with a defined timeline and cross-functional evaluation team. The process sequence, read from the call history and Next Step, is: (1) Discovery and scoping (2026-02-17 call with Gajendra, Munaf, and procurement team). (2) RFI/RFP responses and vendor demos (2026-03-12 and 2026-03-26 calls: module walkthroughs for P2P, CLM, Expense; Zycus committed to providing demo videos, ROI business cases, and implementation plans under NDA by early April). (3) Use-case validation and technical deep-dives (2026-04-28: AP and supplier-portal workflows with Janice; 2026-06-16: AI redlining and clause-extraction session with Karishma, legal, and finance stakeholders). (4) Proposal submission (2026-06-23 per Next Step 2026-06-24). (5) Vendor shortlist finalization by 2026-07-15 (champion Gajendra's target per Next Step). (6) Final selection and commercial approval (implied post-July 15, but the Economic Buyer and approval chain are not documented). The evaluation team is cross-functional: procurement (Gajendra, Munaf), legal/contracts (Karishma), AP (Janice), finance (Melissa Harris on 2026-06-16 call), and one additional stakeholder (Marci Jasinski, role unclear). However, no senior executive (VP or C-level) has joined any call, and the approval gate beyond Gajendra's shortlist recommendation is unmapped. The Next Step notes 'Champion needs to wrap up vendor selection by July 15' but does not name who provides final commercial sign-off or the timeline for MSA/SOW negotiation, InfoSec review, or legal contracting. The process up to shortlist is transparent and on-schedule; the process from shortlist to signature is invisible and at high risk given the 17-day close runway.

decision_criteria.narrative: Russell Investments has run a structured, multi-criteria evaluation with explicit requirements captured across discovery, RFI/RFP scoping, and use-case validation sessions. The confirmed criteria, in priority order based on call emphasis and explicit asks, are: (1) AI and automation capability, particularly for contract redlining, clause extraction, and spend analytics (Karishma's request for a live AI redlining demo on 2026-06-16; multiple stakeholders asked about open-source AI integration support for custom agent development, signaling a strategic AI-first posture). (2) End-to-end P2P + CLM + Expense coverage with tight AP and finance-system integration (modules in scope: iContract, iSource, ML iAnalyze, iSave, Merlin, Merlin Intake per Salesforce Products field; Janice from AP asked about invoice-matching and payment workflows on 2026-04-28). (3) Usability and adoption benchmarks: the team requested region-specific (Americas, EMEA, APAC) and BFSI-industry adoption data on 2026-03-26, indicating peer-benchmarking as a formal decision input. (4) Implementation and integration feasibility: questions on supplier-portal architecture and ERP integration touchpoints appeared on the 2026-04-28 and 2026-06-16 calls. (5) Roadmap transparency and AI product maturity: the team requested a 2026-2027 product roadmap document with tentative release timelines (2026-06-16), signaling concern about buying on promise vs. current capability. Two criteria remain partially validated: the live AI redlining demo was requested but delivery is unconfirmed, and open-source AI integration support was asked about but the answer is not on record. These are late-stage validation items that may anchor the shortlist decision.

ai_fit_signal.summary: No AIS (AI Score/Status/Why) data in Salesforce for this deal, so AI fit cannot be benchmarked against the field-based excitement tier. However, the Avoma call evidence shows Russell Investments is AI Hungry: (1) Karishma (legal) on 2026-06-16 explicitly requested a live working version of AI redlining assistance and asked about AI-driven clause extraction and global contract standardization, signaling AI is a priority decision criterion, not a nice-to-have. (2) A stakeholder on the same call asked whether Zycus supports open-source AI integration for building custom agents on the platform, indicating they are evaluating AI extensibility and strategic AI investment, not just turnkey features. (3) Merlin (AI contract assistant) and Merlin Intake (agentic sourcing) are both in-scope per Salesforce Products field, confirming AI modules are part of the commercial proposal. (4) The evaluation team requested a 2026-2027 product roadmap with release timelines (2026-06-16), suggesting concern about AI maturity and a desire to buy on current capability rather than promise. This is a deal where AI is a competitive wedge and a validation gate, and the buyer is sophisticated enough to ask for live proof vs. roadmap. Tier: AI Hungry. Risk: Zycus has not yet delivered the live AI demo Karishma requested, so the appetite is confirmed but the positioning is incomplete.
